flashcards.front: What is cache memory and why is it used?
flashcards.back: Cache memory is a small, fast memory located between the CPU and main memory. It stores frequently accessed data and instructions to reduce the memory access time, bridging the speed gap between the fast CPU and slower main memory.
Temporal locality means that if a memory location is accessed once, it is likely to be accessed again soon. Recently accessed items have a high probability of being accessed again.
Spatial locality means that if a memory location is accessed, nearby memory locations are likely to be accessed soon. Programs tend to access consecutive memory locations in sequence.
A cache hit occurs when the CPU requests data that is found in the cache memory. The data is delivered quickly without accessing the slower main memory.
A cache miss occurs when the CPU requests data that is not found in the cache. The system must then fetch the data from main memory, which takes significantly longer.

flashcards.front: What is direct mapping in cache?
flashcards.back: Direct mapping assigns each memory block to a specific cache line using the formula: cache line = (block address) mod (number of cache lines). It is simple but can cause conflicts if multiple blocks map to the same line.

flashcards.front: What is associative mapping in cache?
flashcards.back: Associative mapping allows any memory block to be placed in any cache line. It provides the best hit rate but requires expensive hardware to search all cache lines in parallel.
Set-associative mapping combines direct and associative mapping. Cache is divided into sets, each containing multiple lines. A block can be placed in any line within a specific set.

flashcards.front: What is the LRU replacement policy?
flashcards.back: LRU (Least Recently Used) replaces the block that has not been accessed for the longest time. It is effective for temporal locality but requires additional hardware to track usage.
Write-through writes data to both cache and main memory simultaneously. It ensures data consistency but increases memory write time.
Write-back writes data only to the cache initially. The main memory is updated only when the cache block is replaced. It is faster but requires maintaining a dirty bit.
Cache coherence ensures that multiple caches have consistent data. In multiprocessor systems, when one cache modifies data, other caches must be notified to invalidate or update their copies.

flashcards.front: What is hit rate in cache?
flashcards.back: Hit rate is the fraction of memory accesses that find data in cache. It is calculated as (number of hits) / (total memory accesses). A higher hit rate means better performance.

flashcards.front: What is the average memory access time formula?
flashcards.back: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) = Hit time + (Miss rate × Miss penalty). This formula helps evaluate cache performance by considering both hits and misses.

flashcards.front: What are the three levels of cache?
flashcards.back: L1 cache is the smallest and fastest, located on the CPU chip. L2 cache is larger and slower, often on a separate chip. L3 cache is the largest and slowest, shared among multiple cores.
